title: Add corrective scope guardrail to developer agent steering file
description:
Background: corrective beads (`bead_id` ending in `-corrective` or `-corrective-N`) are auto-generated by the scheduler when a parent bead blocks on a transient failure. Their job is to fix the specific failure so the parent can be retried. They should NOT add unrelated improvements, refactors, or additional fixes the agent happens to notice.

Observed in 2026-05-05 session: merge-conflict bead `B-24db26cb`'s corrective was supposed to add DU conflict handling to `_resolve_bead_state_conflicts`, but it ALSO reapplied a deeper `merge_main_into_branch` fix that had previously been reverted. That second, out-of-scope change introduced new regressions and contributed to a multi-hour merge tangle. The corrective's review approved the work because it solved the immediate blocker; nothing in the agent steering said "don't expand scope."

This bead adds a soft guardrail in the developer agent's steering file. No mechanical enforcement — that is deliberately deferred. If this soft guardrail proves insufficient, a follow-up spec can add `expected_files` inheritance from parent → corrective with a finalize-time scope check.

## Required change

In `templates/agents/developer.md` (the canonical developer agent guardrail template), add a section near the top of the body — before the existing scope/style guidance — explaining the corrective-scope rule. Suggested wording:

```markdown
## Corrective bead scope

If your `bead_id` ends in `-corrective` or `-corrective-<N>` (e.g. `B-abc12345-corrective`, `B-abc12345-test-corrective-2`), you are running as a CORRECTIVE bead. Your scope is **strictly limited to fixing the specific failure that blocked the parent bead**. Do not:

- Add unrelated improvements, refactors, or cleanup, even if you notice them
- Reapply previously-reverted changes (the operator reverted them for a reason)
- Expand the surface area beyond what is needed to unblock the parent
- Touch files outside what the parent bead's `expected_files` / `expected_globs` covered, unless the failure is genuinely in another file and there is no smaller fix

If you notice an unrelated issue while fixing the corrective scope: **file a separate bead** via `takt bead create` describing what you saw, but do not fix it in this corrective. The operator will prioritize and dispatch.

When in doubt about scope: prefer the smallest possible fix. The reviewer of this corrective will check that the scope was respected.
```

The exact placement and phrasing can be tightened during implementation; the substance is what matters.

## Acceptance criteria

1. `templates/agents/developer.md` has a "Corrective bead scope" section (or equivalent heading) covering the four "do not" bullets above plus the "file a separate bead" guidance.
2. The section is placed prominently — near the top of the body, before generic implementation guidance — so agents loading the template see it before they start work.
3. No code changes outside `templates/agents/developer.md`. This is a steering-file-only change.
4. `uv run pytest tests/ -n auto -q` passes (no regressions; the change is to a markdown template, no test impact expected).
5. Other agent templates (tester.md, documentation.md, review.md, planner.md, recovery.md) are NOT modified — correctives are always developer-typed in current takt design.

## Out of scope

- Mechanical enforcement (`expected_files` inheritance, finalize-time scope checks). Reserved for a follow-up if this soft guardrail proves insufficient in real usage.
- Updating CLAUDE.md or other operator docs. The change here is for the AGENT, not the operator.
- Changing how the scheduler creates corrective beads.
- Renaming or restructuring the existing `templates/agents/` files.

## References

- `templates/agents/developer.md` — the file to modify
- CLAUDE.md "Corrective beads" subsection in Key Concepts (background on how correctives are created)
- Session lesson from 2026-05-05: merge-conflict bead `B-24db26cb`'s corrective expanded scope and broke the merge — the case study that motivated this bead
